flashcards.front: What is the FIFO principle in queue data structures?
flashcards.back: FIFO stands for First-In-First-Out. It is the fundamental principle of a queue where the first element inserted is the first one to be removed. Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front.

flashcards.front: What is the Enqueue operation in a queue?
flashcards.back: Enqueue adds an element to the rear (back) of the queue. In array implementation, we first check if the queue is not full, then increment rear and store the value at queue[rear]. If front is -1, we also set front to 0.

flashcards.front: What is the Dequeue operation in a queue?
flashcards.back: Dequeue removes and returns the element from the front of the queue. We first check if the queue is not empty, then retrieve the value at queue[front] and increment front. If front exceeds rear, we reset both indices to -1.

flashcards.front: What is the Peek operation and when is it used?
flashcards.back: Peek returns the element at the front of the queue without removing it. It is used when we want to check which element is next in line without actually processing it. The queue state remains unchanged after this operation.

flashcards.front: What is memory wastage in a linear queue and why does it occur?
flashcards.back: Memory wastage occurs because the front index keeps moving forward but never backward. After several dequeues, even though slots at the beginning are empty, new elements cannot be added when rear reaches MAX-1, causing overflow despite available space.

flashcards.front: How does a Queue differ from a Stack?
flashcards.back: Queue follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out): insert at rear, delete from front. Stack follows LIFO (Last-In-First-Out): insert and delete both at the top. Queue is used for BFS and scheduling; Stack is used for DFS and recursive calls.

flashcards.front: What are the main types of queues?
flashcards.back: Four main types: (1) Linear Queue - basic FIFO with front and rear ends; (2) Circular Queue - rear wraps around to front reusing empty slots; (3) Deque - insertion/deletion at both ends; (4) Priority Queue - elements removed by priority not arrival order.

flashcards.front: Name at least three real-world applications of queues.
flashcards.back: CPU Scheduling (processes wait in ready queue), Breadth-First Search (BFS) in graphs, Print Spooling (documents queued for printing), Buffering I/O devices, Disk Scheduling, and Call Center call management.
